---
title: "Client Report - Famous Names"
subtitle: "Unit 1 Task 3"
author: "[STUDENT NAME]"
format:
  html:
    self-contained: true
    page-layout: full
    title-block-banner: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: body
    number-sections: false
    html-math-method: katex
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show the code"
    code-overflow: wrap
    code-copy: hover
    code-tools:
        source: false
        toggle: true
        caption: See code
execute: 
  warning: false
    
---

```{python}
import polars as pl
import numpy as np
from lets_plot import *

LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)
```

```{python}
# Learn more about Code Cells: https://quarto.org/docs/reference/cells/cells-jupyter.html

# Load the wide names dataset used throughout the report.
raw_df = pl.read_csv("https://posit.byui.edu/content/0d43d66f-d3b7-4c43-8c36-52e3ed69e7f1/names_year_csv.csv")

df = raw_df.with_columns(
  [pl.col(column).cast(pl.Int64) for column in raw_df.columns if column != "name"]
  + [pl.col("name").str.to_titlecase()]
)

state_columns = [column for column in df.columns if column not in ["name", "year", "Total"]]
```



## QUESTION 1

<!-- Make sure at least one of your charts includes reference marks!! -->

__Make a new column that represents the total number of babies with your name for only those states in which you have lived. (If you have only ever lived in one state, pick at least one other state so you get practice summing across columns). Make a graph displaying that total for your name over time.__

I used Vincent and combined Idaho and Utah as an example of summing across multiple state columns. The chart shows the total number of babies with that name in the selected states over time, and the dashed line marks the peak year.

```{python}
# Q1
my_name = "Vincent"
lived_states = ["ID", "UT"]

q1 = (
  df
  .filter(pl.col("name") == my_name)
  .select(
    "year",
    pl.sum_horizontal(*[pl.col(column) for column in lived_states]).alias("lived_states_total")
  )
  .sort("year")
)

q1_peak = q1.sort("lived_states_total", descending=True).head(1)
peak_year = int(q1_peak["year"][0])
peak_count = int(q1_peak["lived_states_total"][0])

ggplot(q1, aes(x="year", y="lived_states_total")) + \
  geom_line(color="#1f4e79", size=1.1) + \
  geom_point(data=q1_peak, mapping=aes(x="year", y="lived_states_total"), color="#d94841", size=3.5) + \
  geom_vline(xintercept=peak_year, linetype="dashed", color="#d94841", size=0.9) + \
  labs(
    title=f"Usage of {my_name} in the Selected States Over Time",
    subtitle=f"States combined: {', '.join(lived_states)}; peak year: {peak_year} ({peak_count})",
    x="Year",
    y="Total Babies"
  ) + \
  scale_x_continuous(format=".0f") + \
  theme_minimal()


```


## QUESTION 2

1. __Let's look at a name that is special for members of the church: Brigham. For each year, calculate what proportion of babies named Brigham were born in Idaho and Utah? Make a graph displaying the proportion over time.__  

For Brigham, Idaho and Utah account for the whole total in some years and a smaller share in others. The proportion is always between 0 and 1 because it is a share of the national Brigham total.

```{python}
# Q2

q2 = (
  df
  .filter(pl.col("name") == "Brigham")
  .select(
    "year",
    (pl.col("ID") + pl.col("UT")).alias("idaho_utah_total"),
    pl.col("Total").alias("national_total")
  )
  .with_columns(
    (pl.col("idaho_utah_total") / pl.col("national_total")).alias("proportion")
  )
  .sort("year")
)

ggplot(q2, aes(x="year", y="proportion")) + \
  geom_line(color="#2a6f97", size=1.1) + \
  geom_point(color="#2a6f97", size=2.2) + \
  geom_hline(yintercept=0.5, linetype="dashed", color="#b35c00", size=0.9) + \
  labs(
    title="Share of Brigham Births in Idaho and Utah",
    subtitle="Dashed line marks a 50% reference level",
    x="Year",
    y="Proportion of U.S. Brigham Births"
  ) + \
  scale_x_continuous(format=".0f") + \
  theme_minimal()


```

<!-- Make sure at least one of your charts includes reference marks!! -->

## QUESTION 3

1. __Consider doing some data validation. For the name David, calculate a sum of all 50 states and District of Columbia for each year. Now compare that total to the value in the 'Total' column. In which years, if any, do they not agree?__

The validation check shows that the statewide sum for David matches the Total column in every year. There are no mismatching years.

```{python}
# Q3

q3 = (
  df
  .filter(pl.col("name") == "David")
  .select(
    "year",
    pl.sum_horizontal(*[pl.col(column) for column in state_columns]).alias("state_sum"),
    pl.col("Total").alias("total")
  )
  .with_columns(
    (pl.col("state_sum") - pl.col("total")).alias("difference")
  )
  .sort("year")
)

validation_summary = q3.select(
  pl.len().alias("years_checked"),
  (pl.col("difference") == 0).sum().alias("years_matching"),
  (pl.col("difference") != 0).sum().alias("years_mismatching")
)

validation_summary

q3.filter(pl.col("difference") != 0)


```
